title: Error: writing blob: storing blob to file "xyz": happened during read: unexpected EOF
body:
### Issue Description

I keep getting an error when trying to pull a large image (one layer is 4Gib, another is 6Gib).  The error happens when the smaller of the 2 layers is done downloading:

`Error: writing blob: storing blob to file "/var/tmp/container_images_storage2182552442/3": happened during read: unexpected EOF (after reconnecting, server did not process a Range: header, status 200)`


### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. podman pull quay.io/rh-aiservices-bu/instructlab-workbench-code-server-cuda:0.20.1
2. 
3.


### Describe the results you received

$  podman pull quay.io/rh-aiservices-bu/instructlab-workbench-code-server-cuda:0.20.1
Trying to pull quay.io/rh-aiservices-bu/instructlab-workbench-code-server-cuda:0.20.1...

Error: writing blob: storing blob to file "/var/tmp/container_images_storage2182552442/3": happened during read: unexpected EOF (after reconnecting, server did not process a Range: header, status 200)


### Describe the results you expected

success message that the image has been pulled correctly.

comments.body:
@mtrmac @giuseppe is a 200 really an error case after reconnecting?
https://github.com/containers/image/blob/main/docker/body_reader.go#L189-L190

Interestingly enough, when I issued the same command but used 'docker pull' instead of 'podman pull' it worked fine. I don't have docker installed on my machine so it's just emulating the docker CLI. (I have the podman-docker package installed).   

```
docker pull quay.io/rh-aiservices-bu/instructlab-workbench-code-server-cuda:0.20.1
Emulate Docker CLI using podman. Create /etc/containers/nodocker to quiet msg.
Trying to pull quay.io/rh-aiservices-bu/instructlab-workbench-code-server-cuda:0.20.1...
Getting image source signatures
Copying blob 794b0d054e33 skipped: already exists  
Copying blob 3caea7c6ac39 skipped: already exists  
Copying blob 7b90dc06979e skipped: already exists  
Copying blob ed7908a4c38d done   | 
Copying blob 452e045dbfd1 done   | 
Copying blob 54b43ea517f5 skipped: already exists  
Copying blob 3f2224cce134 skipped: already exists  
Copying blob 26de6be27031 skipped: already exists  
Copying blob e45fec053d69 done   | 
Copying blob 634dc8481493 done   | 
Copying blob bcdd67bedaa0 done   | 
Copying blob 7db25bfead20 skipped: already exists  
Copying blob 7ea4f4508aeb done   | 
Copying blob 3771c562bfc6 done   | 
Copying blob 358a0f6381a0 done   | 
Copying blob 873d5e44ed31 done   | 
Copying blob 62419e3d5b20 done   | 
Copying blob f6e014340041 done   | 
Copying blob 90908ff80093 done   | 
Copying blob 4b926201b61e done   | 
Copying blob 64d604bee652 done   | 
Copying blob f6c4c63fe4bc done   | 
Copying config a212c69bae done   | 
Writing manifest to image destination
a212c69bae633a1f8feb03cd79434c37cf888b7369e15327a30ecf0b065a2381
```

> is a 200 really an error case after reconnecting? https://github.com/containers/image/blob/main/docker/body_reader.go#L189-L190

Yes, the code says why. (Alternatively, if we see an error after reading 10 GB of data, we could re-read and discard that 10 GB of data, but that seems very wasteful, and, assuming the link reliability is a constant, ~50% likely to fail. In the worst case it might end up transferring O(N^2) bytes.)

> Interestingly enough, when I issued the same command but used 'docker pull' instead of 'podman pull' it worked fine.

That’s not starting from a fresh store, is it? The “already exists” layers have been pulled previously. So, if the link/server is unreliable _just the right amount_ (enough that the existing retry code refuses to try again, or failing in a way the retry code rejects — but not so unreliable that a layer pull never finishes), it’s possible that repeated pulls are going to make progress, eventually succeeding to pull an image.

I re-ran the same pull command with the `podman pull` about 5 times (which is why you see that most of the smaller layers had already been pulled previously). The problem was that there were 2 very large layers, one of 4+GiB and one of 6+GiB, and each time when the 4GiB layer **finished** downloading, I would get the error shown above. So 5 times out of 5, the exact same behavior.  And each time when the download finished, not during the download, but at the end. So I don't believe the error was caused by an unstable uplink, but rather by the system writing the result away.  Could there have been some sort of weird buffering issue?  

Then I tried with `docker pull`, I expected the same result since it's just emulating the Docker CLI, however for some reason this time it succeeded.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
